key_insight: The paper argues that AI-native biotechs should organize around a shared predictive "Company World Model" of asset-to-value transitions, rather than copying human department charts into agent roles.
clinical_relevance: This is a strategy and operations study for drug-development organizations, not evidence that AI systems improve patient outcomes, clinical efficacy, or real-world drug success.
method: Built a dry-lab benchmark with 45 retrospective public-information decision cases and strict time cutoffs.
Compared four organizational designs: human-org-mimic, stronger human-org-mimic-plus, AI-native asset-centric, and AI-native value-conversion architectures.
Used automatic scoring and blinded pairwise judging under a success function spanning BD, regulatory approval/launch, and revenue discipline.
evidence_signal: The value-conversion architecture achieved the highest automatic value-conversion score and was preferred by value-specific blinded judges, but a stronger human baseline remained competitive and a neutral judge did not show robust dominance.
watch_item: The benchmark is dry-lab only, so the results may not transfer to real company performance or support claims about actual drug, regulatory, or commercial success.
paper_title: Do AI-Native Biotechs Need Departments? Benchmarking Company World Models for AI-Driven Drug Development
